title: Apple's Power Mac G5 Hits the Street
author: Jay Lyman, TechNewsWorld
abstract: TechNewsWorld article (Aug 18 2003, Jay Lyman) on Apple's shipment of the Power Mac G5 — the first personal computer with 64-bit processing and a 1-GHz front-side bus, billed as 'world's fastest personal computer.' IBM-Apple PowerPC G5 (PowerPC 970). Configurations: 1.6 GHz at $2,000, 1.8 GHz at $2,400, dual 2.0 GHz at $3,000. Apple reports 100,000+ pre-orders since June 23 introduction. Aberdeen chief research officer Peter Kastner provides two of the article's most-quoted lines: 'For a new chip, this is about as good as it gets. The performance improvements are so evident that only a few minutes in the store demo area will convince many buyers that it's time to upgrade.' And: 'The G5 is a hot box and one which for the first time in several years gives Mac owners some bragging rights versus their industry-standard brethren.' Kastner calls the G5 'better, faster, cheaper, smaller' for Apple and predicts graphics designers will see vast improvements from 64-bit-optimized code. IDC's Alan Promisel: 'It's definitely an architecture the commercial IT managers are keeping an eye on.'
importance_rationale: Documents the Aug 2003 Power Mac G5 launch — Apple's first 64-bit machine, 3-year PowerPC peak, the high-water mark of Apple-IBM PowerPC cooperation, and the hardware platform Apple would abandon for Intel less than 3 years later. Kastner quoted twice in the article headlines capture the contemporary buyer enthusiasm.
relevance_rationale: The G5 era is now historical — Apple moved to Intel 2006, to Apple Silicon 2020. But the architectural-transition / bragging-rights pattern (chip architecture repositions the premium PC category) recurs: Apple Silicon M1-M4 (2020-2024) reclaimed performance-per-watt leadership over Intel/AMD for laptops.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's enthusiasm proved half-validated. The G5 delivered a real performance bump in Aug 2003 and justified his 'about as good as it gets' framing. But IBM failed to deliver the promised 3 GHz G5 within a year (still 2.5 GHz through 2005), and heat/power issues made G5 unshippable in laptops — triggering Apple's WWDC 2005 Intel transition announcement. 'Bragging rights for the first time in several years' proved short-lived.
